eli3: A z-test asks: if the null hypothesis were true, how surprising is the data we observed? It standardises the test statistic by subtracting the null-hypothesis mean and dividing by the known standard error — converting the result to a z-score on the standard normal distribution.

You use a z-test when you know the population standard deviation σ (rare in practice) or when the sample is large enough (n ≥ 30) that the CLT guarantees x̄ is approximately normal and s ≈ σ. In practice, the t-test is almost always used instead — it accounts for estimating σ from the data.
The one-sample z-test: z = (x̄ − μ₀) / (σ/√n). The p-value is the probability of observing |z| or more extreme under H₀. Reject H₀ if |z| > 1.96 (two-sided, α=0.05) or if p-value < 0.05.

whenWhy.title: Testing a population mean when σ is known
whenWhy.body: The classical setting: test H₀: μ = μ₀ with known σ. Example: a factory knows the historical σ of weights and wants to test whether a new batch has a different mean. The z-test is the most powerful test in this setting by the Neyman-Pearson lemma (UMP test for the normal family).

whenWhy.title: Large-sample approximation (σ estimated)
whenWhy.body: For n ≥ 30, s ≈ σ and the z-test is approximately valid even with estimated σ (by CLT + Slutsky's theorem). Many online A/B testing calculators use the z-test for conversion rates (proportion test) because with millions of observations, σ is essentially known from the proportion itself: σ = √(p(1−p)).

whenWhy.title: Two-proportion z-test for A/B testing
whenWhy.body: H₀: p₁ = p₂ (e.g. two versions of a webpage have equal conversion rates). Pooled SE = √[p̂(1−p̂)(1/n₁ + 1/n₂)] where p̂ = (x₁+x₂)/(n₁+n₂). z = (p̂₁−p̂₂)/SE. For very large n (millions of users), normal approximation is exact and z-test is the right tool.

whenWhy.title: Benchmark: teaches core logic of hypothesis testing
whenWhy.body: The z-test introduces all concepts used in every subsequent test: null hypothesis, test statistic, sampling distribution, p-value, significance level, rejection region, Type I and Type II errors, one-sided vs two-sided tests. Mastering the z-test is the foundation for understanding ANOVA, chi-square, and likelihood ratio tests.

deepDiveIntro: For normally distributed data with known population variance, the test statistic is the sample mean minus the hypothesized mean divided by the population standard error. Under the null hypothesis this follows a standard normal distribution. The null is rejected for two-sided tests when the absolute value of the statistic exceeds the critical value for the chosen significance level.

The z-test is the uniformly most powerful test for the normal location family with known variance, as shown by the Neyman-Pearson lemma via Karlin-Rubin. Power increases with the true effect size and the square root of the sample size, and decreases with the population standard deviation.
formula: Z = (x̄ − μ₀) / (σ / √n)  ~  N(0,1)  under H₀

deepDive.heading: One-Sample Z-Test
deepDive.body: The one-sample z-test compares a sample mean to a hypothesized population mean when the population standard deviation is known or treated as effectively known. It works by converting the observed deviation into standard-error units and then comparing that result with the standard normal distribution. This is the cleanest textbook example of hypothesis testing with a known null distribution.

deepDive.heading: Two-Sample Z-Test
deepDive.body: The two-sample z-test compares two independent group means when the group variances are known. It standardizes the difference between sample means using the combined sampling variability from both groups. Closely related ideas are used in large-sample proportion tests, especially in online experiments and quality control.

deepDive.heading: Type I Error, Type II Error, and Power
deepDive.body: The z-test fits directly into the usual framework of false positives, false negatives, and statistical power. Once you specify a threshold for rejection, you fix the long-run false-positive rate and can then analyze how sample size and effect size determine the chance of detection. This makes the z-test a useful prototype for power calculations.

deepDive.heading: p-value Interpretation
deepDive.body: The p-value from a z-test quantifies how unusual the observed standardized deviation would be if the null hypothesis were exactly correct. It is a measure of incompatibility with the null model, not a direct measure of effect size or scientific importance. That is why practical interpretation always needs the estimate itself and its uncertainty interval.

takeaways: Z = (x̄−μ₀)/(σ/√n) ~ N(0,1) under H₀; requires known σ or n ≥ 30 by CLT. [1]
Two-sided test: reject if |Z| > z_{α/2} (1.96 at α=0.05); p = 2·Φ(−|Z|). [1]
UMP test for normal location family with known σ by Neyman-Pearson lemma. [1]
Power = 1−β increases with sample size, effect size (δ), and significance level α. [1]
p-value ≠ P(H₀ is true); it is P(data this extreme | H₀ true). [1][2]
In practice, use t-test instead (σ unknown); z-test is appropriate for A/B testing with very large n. [1]

edgeCases.q: When exactly should you use a z-test vs a t-test?
edgeCases.a: Use z-test when: σ is truly known (rare), or n is very large (≥1000 in practice) and you're comfortable treating s as σ. Use t-test whenever: σ must be estimated from the data, or n < 30. For n ≥ 30 with estimated σ, both give nearly identical results — the t-distribution approaches the normal as degrees of freedom → ∞. In practice, default to the t-test. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the connection between the z-test and confidence intervals?
edgeCases.a: Duality: the hypothesis H₀: μ = μ₀ is rejected at level α by a two-sided z-test ⟺ μ₀ falls outside the 100(1−α)% CI [x̄ ± z_{α/2}·σ/√n]. The CI inverts the test: it is the set of all null hypothesis values not rejected by the z-test. This duality holds generally for any pivotal test statistic. [1]

sources.label: Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 8
Wasserstein & Lazar (2016) — ASA Statement on p-values, The American Statistician 70(2)
